docs.title: forge3d
docs.text: Pure-Python 3D physics game engine — easy like pygame, beautiful like simulation.

docs.title: What is forge3d?
docs.text:
forge3d is a batteries-included 3D physics engine written entirely in Python — no MuJoCo, no PyBullet, no Bullet. Dynamics, collision, and contact are all solved by forge3d's own code, backed by NumPy and optional JAX acceleration.

import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))
world.add_ground()
box = world.add_box(size=(1, 1, 1), position=(0, 0, 5), mass=1.0)

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world, max_frames=90)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step(dt=1/60)
    viewer.draw()

print(f"Box final z = {box.position[2]:.2f} m")

docs.title: Two faces, one API
docs.text:
Mode Purpose Speed Realtime (Viewer) Interactive simulation, game loop 60 FPS High-Quality (Recorder) Offline video, research figures Seconds / frame

The physics code never changes — only the renderer does. That is the SceneSnapshot contract.

docs.title: Feature highlights
docs.text: Category Details Physics Rigid-body (RNEA / CRBA / ABA), semi-implicit Euler, SAT OBB-OBB, sphere, capsule, GJK/EPA Contact Impulse PGS solver (10 iterations), Coulomb friction, Baumgarte, angular impulse Rendering Real-time PBR (OpenGL via moderngl); HQ software ray-tracer; heightfield terrain rendering Joints Hinge, Prismatic, Ball, Fixed, Distance, Spring — serialized in World.save/load Robots UR5 FK/IK, DH parameters, Jacobian RL Gymnasium-compatible ReachEnv / PickPlaceEnv Performance JAX JIT + vmap: 2,000× throughput; AABB broad-phase; contact cache (no double detection) Typed Full py.typed — works with mypy, pyright

docs.title: New in v1.1.0 — Game-Ready
docs.text: Feature API Static bodies everywhere add_box(static=True), add_capsule(static=True), add_static_box() Runtime physics properties body.friction = 0.1, body.restitution = 0.9 Per-body damping body.linear_damping = 1.0, body.angular_damping = 2.0 Terrain rendering add_terrain() now appears in Viewer as a shaded mesh Local-frame camera FollowCamera(frame="local") — always follows from behind a vehicle Raycast world.raycast(origin, direction, max_dist) → RayHit pygame input bridge InputBuilder.feed_pygame_event(event) HUD text viewer.draw_text(text, x, y, size, color) Weld rotation world.weld(child, parent, local_rotation=...) Joint save/load World.save() now includes all joint types

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: forge3d is built around one principle: physics and rendering never know about each other.

docs.title: The SceneSnapshot contract
docs.text:
world.step()                     # pure physics, no renderer import
snap = world.snapshot()          # SceneSnapshot — pure data
frame = renderer.render(snap)    # renderer consumes data only

The SceneSnapshot is the sole bridge between physics and rendering. It contains:

  • Per-body transform matrices (position + orientation)
  • Shape descriptors (box/sphere/capsule/mesh)
  • Material parameters (colour, roughness, metallic, texture)
  • Camera and light configuration

Swap the renderer without touching the physics:

Physics core        → SceneSnapshot →  RealtimeRenderer (OpenGL)
(RNEA/CRBA/ABA)                    →  HQRenderer       (ray-tracer)
                                   →  headless / training (no snapshot)

docs.title: Layer diagram
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     Public API (Facade)                       │
│   World · Body · Shape · Material · App · Viewer · Recorder   │
│         Input · Key · OrbitCamera · FollowCamera              │
└───────────────┬─────────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
     ┌──────────▼──────────┐   ┌──────────▼───────────────────┐
     │  Physics core        │   │   Rendering layer             │
     │  math/               │   │   Renderer(ABC)               │
     │    se3, quaternion   │   │   ├── RealtimeRenderer        │
     │    spatial algebra   │   │   │     moderngl, PBR shader  │
     │  dynamics/           │   │   │     PCF shadow, MSAA      │
     │    RNEA, CRBA, ABA   │   │   └── HQRenderer              │
     │  collision/          │   │         NumPy ray-tracer      │
     │    SAT, GJK/EPA      │   │         Blinn-Phong + AO + AA │
     │    AABB broad-phase  │   └───────────────────────────────┘
     │  contact/            │             ▲
     │    Impulse PGS       │             │  SceneSnapshot
     │    Coulomb friction  │   ──────────┘  (pure data)
     │  sim/                │
     │    PhysicsWorld      │
     │    JAX batch         │
     └─────────────────────┘

docs.title: Module map
docs.text: Module Responsibility forge3d.math SE3, quaternions, spatial vector algebra forge3d.dynamics RNEA, CRBA, ABA (robot dynamics) forge3d.collision SAT OBB-OBB, sphere, capsule, GJK/EPA, AABB broad-phase forge3d.contact Impulse PGS solver, Coulomb friction, Baumgarte forge3d.model URDF loader, DH kinematics, robot config forge3d.sim PhysicsWorld (step, CRUD, snapshot), JAX batch, domain rand forge3d.render Renderer ABC, SceneSnapshot, realtime + HQ renderers forge3d.robot Robot class, UR5 preset, FK/IK forge3d.io OBJ loader, MeshData, convex hull inertia forge3d.facade World, Body, Shape, Material (thin API wrappers) forge3d.viewer Viewer (realtime render loop + input) forge3d.recorder Recorder (video capture) forge3d.app App (game-loop abstraction) forge3d.input Input, Key forge3d.camera OrbitCamera, FollowCamera

docs.title: Design rules (enforced by tests)
docs.text:
1. Physics core never imports render — grep -r "from forge3d.render" src/forge3d/{math,dynamics,collision,contact,model,sim} must return nothing.
  2. Apps use library as external user — apps/ never imports src/forge3d internals (only public forge3d.* API).
  3. In-place mutation forbidden — physics functions take state and return new state.
  4. Both backends identical — ENGINE_BACKEND=numpy and ENGINE_BACKEND=jax produce numerically equal results (within float64 tolerance).

docs.title: Backends
docs.text:
ENGINE_BACKEND=numpy python my_sim.py   # NumPy (default)
ENGINE_BACKEND=jax   python my_sim.py   # JAX JIT + vmap

The physics core (math/, dynamics/, etc.) uses a thin backend.py shim that maps xp.array, xp.dot, etc. to either numpy or jax.numpy. JAX enables JIT compilation and vmap for batched multi-environment rollouts (2,000× throughput).

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.12 or newer
  • NumPy ≥ 1.26, SciPy ≥ 1.12, JAX (CPU wheel)

forge3d runs entirely on CPU — no CUDA, no GPU required for physics or training.

docs.title: pip (recommended)
docs.text:
# Physics core only (no rendering)
pip install pyforge3d

# + Real-time OpenGL and HQ ray-tracer
pip install "pyforge3d[render]"

# + Gymnasium + Stable-Baselines3 RL
pip install "pyforge3d[rl]"

# Everything (for development / research)
pip install "pyforge3d[all]"

Note: The PyPI distribution is named pyforge3d, but the import name remains forge3d: 

import forge3d as f3d   # same as always

docs.title: Development install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/iruki-dev/forge3d.git
cd forge3d
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Headless / server environments
docs.text:
If you run forge3d on a headless server (no display), the realtime renderer uses Xvfb + Mesa llvmpipe (software OpenGL):

sudo apt-get install xvfb libgl1-mesa-dri
export DISPLAY=:99
Xvfb :99 -screen 0 1280x720x24 &
python my_script.py

The HQ ray-tracer has no display dependency — it outputs PNG/MP4 directly.

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
docker compose run --rm dev python examples/01_falling_box_realtime.py

See .devcontainer/docker-compose.yml for the full CPU-only service definition.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Three 15-line examples to get you running immediately.

docs.title: 1. Falling box (realtime)
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))
world.add_ground()
box = world.add_box(size=(1, 1, 1), position=(0, 0, 5), mass=1.0)

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world, max_frames=180)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step(dt=1/60)
    viewer.draw()

print(f"Box landed at z = {box.position[2]:.3f} m")

docs.title: 2. Bouncing ball — high-quality video
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))
world.add_ground(material=f3d.Material(color="ground", roughness=0.8))
world.add_sphere(radius=0.4, position=(0, 0, 4.4), mass=1.0,
                 restitution=0.8, material=f3d.Material(color="orange"))
world.set_camera(position=(4, -7, 3), target=(0, 0, 1))

rec = f3d.Recorder(world, mode="hq", resolution=(1280, 720),
                   samples=16, output="bounce.mp4")
rec.run(duration=3.0, dt=1/240, fps=60)

Same World code — only the renderer changes. That is the SceneSnapshot contract.

docs.title: 3. App-style game loop
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

app = f3d.App("Physics Sandbox", width=1280, height=720, fps=60)
ball = None

@app.on_start
def setup(world: f3d.World) -> None:
    global ball
    world.add_ground()
    ball = world.add_sphere(radius=0.4, position=(0, 0, 6),
                             material=f3d.Material(color="orange"))

@app.on_update
def update(world: f3d.World, dt: float, inp: f3d.Input) -> None:
    if inp.key_pressed(f3d.Key.SPACE):
        world.apply_impulse(ball, (0, 0, 8))

app.run()

docs.title: What's New in v1.1.0 — Game-Ready Release
docs.text: v1.1.0은 게임 제작 경험을 대폭 개선하는 릴리즈입니다. 18개의 개선 요청을 분석해 구현 가능한 항목 15개를 완성했습니다.

docs.title: 🏗️ Static Bodies Everywhere
docs.text:
Before v1.1.0, creating a non-moving collidable box required calling an internal API:

# Old — exposed internal _physics layer
world._physics.add_static_box(size=..., position=..., ...)

Now all body types support static=True and auto-register in world.bodies:

# v1.1.0 — clean public API
wall  = world.add_box(size=(10, 0.5, 3), position=(0, 5, 1.5), static=True)
post  = world.add_capsule(radius=0.15, half_length=3, position=(2,0,3), static=True)
shelf = world.add_static_box(size=(4, 0.2, 0.1), position=(0, 0, 2))

print(wall.is_static)   # True
print(shelf in world.bodies)  # True

Static bodies are excluded from the collision detection outer loop — they don't slow down the simulation.

docs.title: ⚡ Runtime Physics Property Setters
docs.text:
Change material properties after body creation:

box = world.add_box(size=(1,1,1), position=(0,0,5), mass=1.0)

# All settable at runtime
box.friction    = 0.02   # turn surface to ice
box.restitution = 0.95   # make it very bouncy

# Per-body velocity damping — applied automatically each world.step()
box.linear_damping  = 1.0   # exponential decay: 63% removed per second
box.angular_damping = 2.0   # 86% of spin removed per second

Damping is dt-corrected (FPS-independent):

v_next = v × exp(−linear_damping × dt)

docs.title: 🌍 Heightfield Terrain Rendering
docs.text:
world.add_terrain() now renders as a smooth shaded mesh — shadow casting included:

import numpy as np

heights = (np.sin(np.linspace(0, 4*np.pi, 64))[:, None] *
           np.cos(np.linspace(0, 3*np.pi, 64))[None, :] * 3
          ).astype(np.float32)

world.add_terrain(
    heights=np.clip(heights - heights.min(), 0, 8),
    cell_size=2.0,
    origin=(-64, -64, 0),
    material=f3d.Material(color=(0.28, 0.42, 0.16), roughness=0.95),
)

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()   # terrain visible as shaded mesh with shadows ✅

Both RealtimeRenderer (headless/Xvfb) and WindowRenderer (pygame) support terrain rendering.

docs.title: 📷 FollowCamera Local Frame
docs.text:
For vehicle games, the camera should always sit behind the car regardless of direction:

# frame="world" (default): offset stays world-aligned
cam = f3d.FollowCamera(car, offset=(0, -8, 4))
# frame="local": offset rotates with the car body
cam = f3d.FollowCamera(car, offset=(-8, 0, 3), frame="local", smoothing_hz=8)

while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot(dt=viewer.dt))   # dt-corrected smoothing
    viewer.draw()

The smoothing_hz parameter replaces the old per-frame alpha, making behaviour identical at any frame rate.

docs.title: 🔦 Raycast API
docs.text:
Cast a ray and find the first body it hits:

# Ground detection (for suspension simulation)
hit = world.raycast(
    origin=car.position + np.array([0, 0, 0.5]),
    direction=(0, 0, -1),
    max_dist=2.0,
)
on_ground = hit is not None and hit.distance < 0.6

# Line-of-sight check
los = world.raycast(origin=player.position, direction=target_dir, max_dist=50)
can_see_target = los is None or los.distance > 49

Returns a RayHit(body, point, normal, distance) namedtuple or None.

docs.title: 🎮 InputBuilder + pygame Bridge
docs.text:
Use forge3d's Input/Key system in pygame-based games:

import forge3d as f3d

builder = f3d.InputBuilder()

while running:
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        builder.feed_pygame_event(event)   # bridges pygame → f3d

    inp = builder.build()

    # Standard forge3d input API works in pygame games
    if inp.key_held(f3d.Key.W):
        car.apply_force(forward * 500)
    if inp.key_pressed(f3d.Key.R):
        world.teleport(car, start_pos)

    world.step()
    builder.end_frame()

docs.title: 💬 Viewer HUD Text
docs.text:
Render text overlays without a separate UI library:

while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()   # 3D scene

    viewer.draw_text(f"★ {stars}/20", x=10, y=10, size=28,
                     color=(1.0, 0.9, 0.1))
    viewer.draw_text("GAME OVER", x=640, y=360,
                     size=64, anchor="center")
    viewer.draw_text("ESC to quit", x=1270, y=10,
                     size=18, anchor="topright")

docs.title: 🔗 Weld Relative Rotation
docs.text:
world.weld() now preserves relative orientation between child and parent:

hub   = world.add_box(size=(0.5,0.5,0.5), position=(0,0,5), mass=2)
blade = world.add_box(size=(0.3,6,0.2), position=(0,3,5), mass=1)

# Blade spawned at 90° to hub — weld preserves this angle
world.weld(blade, hub)          # relative rotation auto-computed
# Or specify explicitly:
world.weld(blade, hub, local_rotation=[0.707, 0, 0, 0.707])

# Now as hub spins, blades maintain their angles
world.add_joint("hinge", hub, None, axis=(1,0,0), motor_velocity=1.5)

docs.title: 💾 Joint Serialization
docs.text:
World.save() / World.load() now includes all joints:

# Save everything — bodies AND joints
world.save("scene.json")

# Load and continue — joints restored automatically
world2 = f3d.World.load("scene.json")

Supported: HingeJoint, SpringJoint, DistanceJoint, BallJoint, FixedJoint, PrismaticJoint.

docs.title: ⚡ Performance: No Double Collision Detection
docs.text:
In v1.0.0, world.step() called detect_contacts() twice — once for physics, once for event dispatch. v1.1.0 caches contacts from the physics step and reuses them for events:

Before: step() → detect_contacts() [physics] + detect_contacts() [events]
After:  step() → detect_contacts() [physics] → cache → events [free]

This reduces event dispatch overhead by ~50% in event-heavy scenes.

docs.title: Migration from v1.0.0
docs.text:
All changes are backward-compatible. Existing code runs without modification.

Old pattern New pattern (v1.1.0) world._physics.add_static_box(...) world.add_static_box(...) or world.add_box(static=True) Manual car.set_angular_velocity(v * 0.8) each frame car.angular_damping = 1.2 once FollowCamera(body, offset=(0,-8,4)) (world-frame only) Add frame="local" for vehicle tracking alpha=0.1 per-frame smoothing (FPS-dependent) smoothing_hz=6.0 (FPS-independent) No terrain in Viewer Automatic — just world.add_terrain(...) World.save() drops joints Joints now included

docs.title: forge3d.App
docs.text: High-level game-loop abstraction using decorators.

docs.title: App
docs.text: High-level forge3d application with a managed physics + render loop.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: title : Window title (shown in windowed mode). width : Render width in pixels. height : Render height in pixels. fps : Target frames per second; also the physics step rate. gravity : World gravity vector (x, y, z) in m/s².

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: app = f3d.App("My World", fps=60) @app.on_start ... def setup(world): ... world.add_ground() ... world.add_box(position=(0, 0, 5)) @app.on_update ... def update(world, dt, inp): ... if inp.key_held(f3d.Key.W): ... world.apply_impulse(world.bodies[0], (0, 5*dt, 0)) app.run(max_frames=120) # doctest: +SKIP

docs.title: world property
docs.text:
world: Any

The managed :class:~forge3d.facade.World instance.

docs.title: fps property writable
docs.text:
fps: float

Target frames per second.

docs.title: on_start
docs.text:
on_start(func: Callable) -> Callable

Register a callback called once before the game loop begins.

Signature: fn() or fn(world)

docs.title: on_update
docs.text:
on_update(func: Callable) -> Callable

Register a callback called every frame, before :meth:World.step.

Signature: one of: - fn() - fn(world) - fn(world, dt) - fn(world, dt, inp)

docs.title: on_render
docs.text:
on_render(func: Callable) -> Callable

Register a callback called after :meth:Viewer.draw each frame.

Signature: fn() or fn(world) or fn(world, viewer)

docs.title: run
docs.text:
run(max_frames: int | None = None) -> None

Start the game loop.

Initialises the viewer, fires :meth:on_start, then loops: 1. Build :class:~forge3d.input.Input snapshot 2. Call :meth:on_update with (world, dt, inp) 3. world.step(dt) 4. viewer.draw() 5. Fire :meth:on_render if registered 6. Advance frame; stop when max_frames reached or window closed

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: max_frames : Maximum frames to render before stopping automatically. None (default) runs until the window is closed or the default headless limit is reached.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

app = f3d.App("My World", width=1280, height=720, fps=60)
ball = None

@app.on_start
def setup(world: f3d.World) -> None:
    global ball
    world.add_ground()
    ball = world.add_sphere(radius=0.4, position=(0, 0, 6))

@app.on_update
def update(world: f3d.World, dt: float, inp: f3d.Input) -> None:
    if inp.key_pressed(f3d.Key.SPACE):
        world.apply_impulse(ball, (0, 0, 8))

@app.on_render
def render(world: f3d.World, viewer: f3d.Viewer) -> None:
    pass  # custom overlays if needed

app.run()

docs.title: Backend switching (NumPy / JAX)
docs.text:
forge3d supports two computation backends that can be switched at runtime:

ENGINE_BACKEND=numpy python my_script.py   # default
ENGINE_BACKEND=jax   python my_script.py   # JAX JIT + vmap

Both backends produce numerically equivalent results (within float64 tolerance).

docs.title: Why two backends?
docs.text:
• NumPy: Simple, debuggable, no JIT overhead for small scenes.
  • JAX: JIT compilation + vmap batching → 2,000× throughput for RL training.

The JAX backend shines when stepping thousands of environments in parallel:

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from forge3d.sim.jax_batch import batch_reach_reset, batch_reach_step

key = jax.random.PRNGKey(0)
q, tgt, obs = batch_reach_reset(key, n_envs=256)
q, obs, rew, done = batch_reach_step(q, tgt, jnp.zeros((256, 6)))

docs.title: backend
docs.text:
Backend abstraction: numpy ↔ jax.

Select via environment variable before process start

ENGINE_BACKEND=numpy (default) ENGINE_BACKEND=jax

Engine code imports names from here

from forge3d.backend import xp, jit, vmap, set_at, new_prng_key, split_key, rand_uniform

xp — numpy or jax.numpy module jit — jax.jit or no-op decorator vmap — jax.vmap or loop-based fallback

docs.title: backend_name
docs.text:
backend_name() -> str

Return the active backend name ('numpy' or 'jax').

docs.title: jit
docs.text:
jit(fn: Any, **_: Any) -> Any

No-op decorator: functions run eagerly under NumPy.

docs.title: vmap
docs.text:
vmap(fn: Any, in_axes: int = 0, out_axes: int = 0) -> Any

Loop-based vmap fallback: maps fn over axis-0 of all inputs.

docs.title: set_at
docs.text:
set_at(arr: Any, idx: Any, val: Any) -> Any

Return a copy of arr with arr[idx] replaced by val.

docs.title: new_prng_key
docs.text:
new_prng_key(seed: int = 0) -> Any

Create a uint32[2] key compatible with JAX PRNG conventions.

docs.title: split_key
docs.text:
split_key(key: Any, num: int = 2) -> Any

Split key into num independent keys (deterministic, sequential seeds).

docs.title: forge3d.Body
docs.text: A handle to a single rigid body in the simulation. Returned by every World.add_* call.

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
Handle to a simulated rigid body.

Returned by world.add_box(), world.add_sphere(), etc. Use properties to read the body's current state and methods to control it.

collision_layer: int

Bit-field: which layer(s) this body belongs to.

docs.title: collision_mask property writable
docs.text:
collision_mask: int

Bit-field: which layers this body detects collisions with.

docs.title: apply_force
docs.text:
apply_force(force: Any) -> None

Accumulate a world-frame force (N) to apply on the next step.

Forces reset to zero after each :meth:World.step call.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: force : (3,) force vector in Newtons, world frame.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
For per-frame forces use this instead of apply_impulse::

body.apply_force(np.array([0, 0, 50]))   # 50 N upward thrust
world.step(dt=1/60)                       # force applied here

docs.title: apply_torque
docs.text:
apply_torque(torque: Any) -> None

Accumulate a world-frame torque (N·m) to apply on the next step.

Torques reset to zero after each :meth:World.step call.

docs.title: set_position
docs.text:
set_position(position: Any) -> None

Instantly teleport this body to position (keeps orientation).

docs.title: set_orientation
docs.text:
set_orientation(quat: Any) -> None

Instantly set orientation (keeps position). quat = [w, x, y, z].

docs.title: set_velocity
docs.text:
set_velocity(vel: Any) -> None

Override linear velocity (m/s).

docs.title: set_angular_velocity
docs.text:
set_angular_velocity(omega: Any) -> None

Override angular velocity (rad/s).

docs.title: Reading state
docs.text:
box = world.add_box(size=(1,1,1), position=(0,0,5), mass=2.0)
print(box.position)     # array([0., 0., 5.])
print(box.mass)         # 2.0
print(box.is_static)    # False
print(box.is_sleeping)  # False

docs.title: Runtime setters (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
# Change physics material at runtime
box.friction    = 0.05   # ice-slippery (default: 0.5)
box.restitution = 0.95   # very bouncy  (default: 0.3)

docs.title: Velocity damping (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
# Exponential decay — dt-corrected, FPS-independent
box.linear_damping  = 1.0  # removes 63% of speed per second
box.angular_damping = 2.0  # removes 86% of spin per second

# Applied automatically every world.step()
linear_damping Speed remaining after 1 s 0.0 100% (no damping) 0.5 61% 1.0 37% 2.0 14%

docs.title: Applying forces
docs.text:
box.apply_force((0, 0, 50))    # 50 N upward (reset each step)
box.apply_torque((0, 0, 10))   # 10 N·m yaw torque

world.step(dt=1/60)
print(box.velocity)

docs.title: Collision layers
docs.text:
from forge3d import CollisionLayer

player = world.add_sphere(radius=0.5, position=(0,0,3), mass=1)
player.collision_layer = CollisionLayer.PLAYER
player.collision_mask  = CollisionLayer.ALL

ghost = world.add_sphere(radius=1.0, position=(5,0,0), mass=0.1)
ghost.collision_layer = CollisionLayer.NONE   # no collision

docs.title: forge3d.Camera
docs.text: Two camera controllers that produce CameraSnapshot for the Viewer.

docs.title: OrbitCamera
docs.text:
Spherical-coordinate camera that orbits a target point.

The camera is always looking at target from a point on a sphere of radius distance. azimuth (yaw) rotates around the z-axis; elevation (pitch) tilts above/below the horizon.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
target : Point the camera looks at, world-frame (x, y, z). distance : Eye-to-target distance in metres. azimuth : Horizontal angle in degrees (0 = +x axis). elevation : Vertical angle in degrees above the horizon (clamped ±89°). fov_deg : Vertical field-of-view in degrees.

Usage::

cam = f3d.OrbitCamera(target=(0, 0, 1), distance=8)
cam.rotate(d_azimuth=45)         # spin 45° around target
cam.zoom(2)                      # move 20% closer (delta > 0 = closer)
snap = cam.to_snapshot()
viewer.set_camera(snap)

docs.title: position property
docs.text:
position: ndarray

Current world-space eye position.

docs.title: rotate
docs.text:
rotate(
    d_azimuth: float = 0.0, d_elevation: float = 0.0
) -> OrbitCamera

Orbit around the target.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: d_azimuth : Azimuth delta in degrees. d_elevation : Elevation delta in degrees (clamped to ±89°).

docs.title: zoom
docs.text:
zoom(delta: float) -> OrbitCamera

Adjust distance.

delta > 0 moves the camera closer; delta < 0 moves it farther. The factor is distance *= (1 - delta * 0.1) so a delta of 1.0 reduces distance by 10%.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: delta : Zoom speed; typically Input.scroll_delta().

docs.title: set_distance
docs.text:
set_distance(d: float) -> OrbitCamera

Directly set the eye-to-target distance.

docs.title: pan
docs.text:
pan(dx: float, dy: float) -> OrbitCamera

Translate the target point in screen space.

Useful for middle-mouse-button panning.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: dx, dy : Pixel offsets in screen space.

docs.title: look_at
docs.text:
look_at(target: Any) -> OrbitCamera

Point the camera at a new target without changing distance.

docs.title: to_snapshot
docs.text:
to_snapshot() -> CameraSnapshot

Build a :class:~forge3d.render.snapshot.CameraSnapshot from the current orbit state — suitable for :meth:Viewer.set_camera.

docs.title: FollowCamera
docs.text:
Camera that smoothly tracks a :class:~forge3d.facade.Body.

The camera sits at body.position + offset and looks at body.position. Supports world-frame and body-local-frame offsets, and uses dt-corrected exponential smoothing (FPS-independent).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: body : The :class:Body to follow. offset : Camera offset from the body in frame coordinates. frame : "world" (default) — offset is world-aligned; stays fixed regardless of body rotation. "local" — offset is body-local; camera always sits behind the body as it turns (ideal for vehicles). smoothing_hz : Smoothing speed in Hz (reciprocal of time constant τ). Higher = snappier. Default 6 ≈ 0.17 s settling time. Pass smoothing_hz=None for legacy per-frame alpha. alpha : Legacy per-frame lerp factor (ignored when smoothing_hz is set). Kept for backward compatibility. fov_deg : Vertical field-of-view in degrees.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Usage (per-frame)::

viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot(dt=viewer.dt))

docs.title: World-frame offset (original behaviour)
docs.text: cam = f3d.FollowCamera(ball, offset=(0, -8, 4))

docs.title: Body-local offset — always behind the car
docs.text: cam = f3d.FollowCamera(car, offset=(-8, 0, 3), frame="local", ... smoothing_hz=8)

docs.title: to_snapshot
docs.text:
to_snapshot(dt: float | None = None) -> 'CameraSnapshot'

Update smoothed position and return a :class:CameraSnapshot.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: dt : Elapsed time since last call (seconds). When provided, uses dt-corrected exponential smoothing; otherwise falls back to the legacy per-frame alpha factor.

docs.title: OrbitCamera — orbit around a target
docs.text:
cam = f3d.OrbitCamera(target=(0, 0, 1), distance=10, elevation=30)

while viewer.is_open:
    inp = viewer.input
    if inp.mouse_button(1):                     # right-drag to orbit
        dx, dy = inp.mouse_delta()
        cam.rotate(d_azimuth=dx * 0.4, d_elevation=-dy * 0.4)
    cam.zoom(inp.scroll_delta() * 0.5)
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot())
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: FollowCamera — world-frame offset (default)
docs.text:
ball = world.add_sphere(radius=0.5, position=(0,0,3), mass=1)

cam = f3d.FollowCamera(ball, offset=(0, -8, 4), smoothing_hz=6)

while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot(dt=viewer.dt))
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: FollowCamera — local frame (vehicle camera) (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
car = world.add_box(size=(2, 1, 0.5), position=(0, 0, 0.3), mass=20)

# Camera always sits 10 m behind and 3 m above the car,
# regardless of which direction the car is facing.
cam = f3d.FollowCamera(
    car,
    offset=(-10, 0, 3),   # local-frame: X=back, Z=up
    frame="local",
    smoothing_hz=8.0,      # snappier (default: 6.0)
    fov_deg=60.0,
)

while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot(dt=viewer.dt))
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: Smoothing comparison
docs.text: Parameter Meaning Best for frame="world" Offset stays world-aligned Ball, spacecraft, top-down frame="local" Offset rotates with the body Car, plane, FPS smoothing_hz=2 Slow, cinematic lag Cutscenes smoothing_hz=6 Default, natural Most games smoothing_hz=20 Near-instant snap Action games

docs.title: forge3d.Input & Key
docs.text: Per-frame keyboard and mouse state snapshots.

docs.title: Input dataclass
docs.text:
Immutable snapshot of input state for a single rendered frame.

Created by the viewer's event loop; passed to @app.on_update and available via viewer.input after each :meth:Viewer.draw call.

All keys are plain strings — use :class:Key constants or raw lowercase strings ('w', 'space', 'f1').

Mouse buttons: 0 = left, 1 = right, 2 = middle.

docs.title: key_held
docs.text:
key_held(key: str) -> bool

True while the key is physically held down.

docs.title: key_pressed
docs.text:
key_pressed(key: str) -> bool

True only during the first frame the key was pressed.

docs.title: key_released
docs.text:
key_released(key: str) -> bool

True only during the first frame the key was released.

docs.title: any_key_held
docs.text:
any_key_held(*keys: str) -> bool

True if any of the given keys are held.

docs.title: all_keys_held
docs.text:
all_keys_held(*keys: str) -> bool

True if all of the given keys are held simultaneously.

docs.title: mouse_pos
docs.text:
mouse_pos() -> tuple[float, float]

Current cursor position in pixels (x, y) from top-left.

docs.title: mouse_delta
docs.text:
mouse_delta() -> tuple[float, float]

Cursor movement since last frame (dx, dy) in pixels.

docs.title: mouse_button
docs.text:
mouse_button(button: int = 0) -> bool

True while the given mouse button is held (0=left, 1=right, 2=mid).

docs.title: scroll_delta
docs.text:
scroll_delta() -> float

Mouse wheel scroll this frame (positive = scroll up / zoom in).

docs.title: Key
docs.text:
Keyboard key name constants.

All values are lowercase strings that match the names used internally by the viewer's event back-end. Pass them to :meth:Input.key_held etc.

Examples::

inp.key_held(f3d.Key.SPACE)
inp.key_pressed('w')         # raw strings work too

docs.title: InputBuilder module-attribute
docs.text: InputBuilder = _InputBuilder

docs.title: With Viewer (built-in integration)
docs.text:
viewer = f3d.Viewer(world)
while viewer.is_open:
    inp = viewer.input          # updated each draw()
    if inp.key_pressed(f3d.Key.SPACE):
        world.apply_impulse(ball, (0, 0, 8))
    if inp.key_held(f3d.Key.RIGHT):
        world.apply_impulse(ball, (3 * dt, 0, 0))
    dx, dy = inp.mouse_delta()
    cam.rotate(d_azimuth=dx * 0.3)
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: With pygame (via InputBuilder, v1.1.0)
docs.text:
InputBuilder.feed_pygame_event() bridges pygame events into the standard f3d.Input / f3d.Key system, so you can use forge3d's input API in pygame-based games:

import pygame
import forge3d as f3d

builder = f3d.InputBuilder()

clock = pygame.time.Clock()
while True:
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
            break
        builder.feed_pygame_event(event)   # translate to f3d events

    inp = builder.build()

    # Now use the standard forge3d input API
    if inp.key_held(f3d.Key.W):
        car.apply_force(forward * 500)
    if inp.key_pressed(f3d.Key.R):
        world.teleport(car, start_pos)

    world.step()
    builder.end_frame()
    clock.tick(60)

docs.title: Key constants reference
docs.text:
# Letters
f3d.Key.W, f3d.Key.A, f3d.Key.S, f3d.Key.D   # WASD movement

# Arrows
f3d.Key.UP, f3d.Key.DOWN, f3d.Key.LEFT, f3d.Key.RIGHT

# Special
f3d.Key.SPACE, f3d.Key.ESCAPE, f3d.Key.ENTER
f3d.Key.SHIFT, f3d.Key.CTRL, f3d.Key.ALT

# Function keys
f3d.Key.F1 ... f3d.Key.F12

# Raw strings also work
inp.key_held('w')        # same as key_held(f3d.Key.W)
inp.key_pressed('space')

docs.title: Viewer
docs.text: Realtime viewer for a forge3d World.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: world : World instance to observe. mode : "realtime" — only mode implemented (P6 adds HQ). width, height : Frame dimensions in pixels. max_frames : Auto-close after this many draw() calls. None = infinite (useful with a real window; in headless mode defaults to 300 unless overridden). controls : Reserved for interactive controls (None = defaults).

docs.title: is_open property
docs.text:
is_open: bool

True until the viewer is closed or max_frames is reached.

docs.title: frame_count property
docs.text:
frame_count: int

Number of frames rendered so far.

docs.title: input property
docs.text:
input: Input

Per-frame :class:~forge3d.input.Input snapshot.

Updated at the start of each :meth:draw call. In headless mode this always returns an empty (all-False) snapshot.

docs.title: dt property
docs.text:
dt: float

Nominal per-frame time step (1/60 s). Constant; does not measure wall time.

docs.title: __init__
docs.text:
__init__(
    world: World,
    mode: str = "realtime",
    *,
    width: int = 800,
    height: int = 600,
    max_frames: int | None = None,
    controls: Any = None,
) -> None

docs.title: draw
docs.text:
draw() -> np.ndarray | None

Render one frame from the world's current state.

Also updates :attr:input (builds new snapshot from the event queue).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: ndarray or None RGB frame (H, W, 3) uint8, or None after close.

docs.title: draw_text
docs.text:
draw_text(
    text: str,
    x: int = 10,
    y: int = 10,
    size: int = 20,
    color: tuple = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0),
    bg_alpha: float = 0.6,
    anchor: str = "topleft",
) -> None

Render a HUD text overlay on top of the current frame.

Uses pygame font rendering (if pygame is available) to blit text as an OpenGL texture quad. Must be called after :meth:draw.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
text : Text string to display. x, y : Pixel position of the anchor point. size : Font size in pixels. color : RGB colour in [0, 1] (white by default). bg_alpha : Background rectangle alpha [0, 1]. anchor : "topleft" (default), "center" or "topright".

Example::

while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()
    viewer.draw_text(f"Speed: {speed:.0f} m/s", x=10, y=10)

docs.title: set_camera
docs.text:
set_camera(cam: Any) -> None

Set the camera for subsequent draw() calls.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: cam : A :class:~forge3d.render.snapshot.CameraSnapshot or a camera controller such as :class:~forge3d.camera.OrbitCamera (anything with a to_snapshot() method).

docs.title: run
docs.text:
run(
    dt: float | None = None,
    max_frames: int | None = None,
    collect_frames: bool = False,
) -> list[np.ndarray] | None

Convenience: step + draw in a loop until the viewer closes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: dt : Physics step size (default: world.DEFAULT_DT). max_frames : Override the viewer's max_frames for this run. collect_frames: If True, return all rendered frames as a list.

docs.title: step_once
docs.text:
step_once(dt: float | None = None) -> np.ndarray | None

Advance one physics step and draw one frame (useful for debugging).

docs.title: pause
docs.text:
pause() -> None

Pause: subsequent step() calls inside :meth:run are no-ops.

docs.title: resume
docs.text:
resume() -> None

Resume after :meth:pause.

docs.title: close
docs.text:
close() -> None

Release GPU resources and mark the viewer as closed.

docs.title: Recorder
docs.text: Capture a World's simulation as video frames.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: world : World to record. May be None when only run_policy will be used (no free-simulation recording). mode : 'realtime' or 'hq'. resolution : (width, height) in pixels. output : Output file path (mp4 via imageio, or image sequence dir). samples : Ray samples per pixel for HQ mode (ignored in realtime).

docs.title: run
docs.text:
run(
    duration: float,
    dt: float = 1.0 / 240.0,
    fps: float = 60.0,
) -> None

Simulate for duration seconds and save video.

Physics runs at dt timestep; frames are saved at fps rate. So phys_steps_per_frame = round(1/(fps*dt)).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: duration : Simulation duration in seconds. dt : Physics integration step. fps : Output video frame rate.

docs.title: run_policy
docs.text:
run_policy(
    policy: Any,
    env: Any,
    duration: float = 5.0,
    fps: float = 24.0,
    deterministic: bool = True,
    seed: int = 0,
) -> None

Record a trained policy rollout from a Gymnasium-compatible env.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: policy : SB3 model or any object with predict(obs) method. env : Gymnasium env (must support render_mode="rgb_array"). duration : Recording duration in seconds. fps : Output video frame rate. deterministic : Use deterministic policy actions. seed : Env reset seed.

docs.title: Simple render loop
docs.text:
world = f3d.World()
world.add_ground()
world.add_box(size=(1,1,1), position=(0,0,3), mass=1,
              material=f3d.Material(color="red"))

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world, width=1280, height=720)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step(dt=1/60)
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: Viewer controls (default)
docs.text: Action Control Orbit Left-drag Pan Middle-drag Zoom Scroll Close Esc or window X

docs.title: HUD text overlay (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()                       # render 3D scene first

    viewer.draw_text(f"Score: {score}", x=10, y=10, size=24)
    viewer.draw_text("PAUSED", x=640, y=360, size=48,
                     color=(1.0, 0.8, 0.2), anchor="center")
    viewer.draw_text(f"Speed: {speed:.0f} km/h",
                     x=1270, y=10, size=20, anchor="topright")

docs.title: Terrain rendering (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
Terrain added via world.add_terrain() is now automatically rendered:

import numpy as np

heights = np.sin(np.linspace(0, 4*np.pi, 32)).reshape(32, 1) \
        * np.ones((1, 32)) * 2
heights = heights.astype(np.float32)

world.add_terrain(heights=heights, cell_size=2.0, origin=(-32,-32,0),
                  material=f3d.Material(color=(0.28, 0.45, 0.18)))

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()   # terrain visible as shaded mesh with shadows

docs.title: Camera control
docs.text:
cam = f3d.OrbitCamera(target=(0,0,1), distance=10, elevation=30)

while viewer.is_open:
    inp = viewer.input
    if inp.mouse_button(1):
        dx, dy = inp.mouse_delta()
        cam.rotate(d_azimuth=dx*0.5, d_elevation=-dy*0.5)
    cam.zoom(inp.scroll_delta() * 0.5)
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot())
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: HQ video recording
docs.text:
world.set_camera(position=(4, -7, 3), target=(0, 0, 1))

rec = f3d.Recorder(
    world,
    mode="hq",
    resolution=(1920, 1080),
    samples=64,           # rays per pixel (quality)
    output="scene.mp4",
)
rec.run(duration=5.0, dt=1/240, fps=60)

docs.title: forge3d.robot
docs.text: Robot arm loading, FK, IK, and Jacobian.

docs.title: Robot
docs.text:
Kinematic robot arm.

Holds joint angles, computes FK, and exposes link poses for rendering. Physics (dynamics) is NOT simulated — the arm is kinematically controlled.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: model : RigidBodyModel (n revolute joints, serial chain). link_radii : Visual cylinder radius per link (m). Default: 0.04 m. name : Display name. material : Snapshot material ID for link boxes. base_position : World-frame position of the robot base.

docs.title: q property writable
docs.text:
q: ndarray

Current joint angles (rad), shape (n,).

docs.title: set_joint
docs.text:
set_joint(idx: int, angle: float) -> None

Set joint idx to angle (radians).

docs.title: set_joints
docs.text:
set_joints(q: Any) -> None

Set all joint angles at once.

docs.title: link_world_poses
docs.text:
link_world_poses() -> list[tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]]

FK for all links.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: List of (pos, R) pairs (one per link), world frame. pos : (3,) float64 — joint origin position. R : (3,3) float64 — link frame orientation.

docs.title: ee_pose
docs.text:
ee_pose() -> tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]

End-effector pose (last link): (pos, R) in world frame.

docs.title: link_visual_boxes
docs.text:
link_visual_boxes() -> list[
    tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray]
]

Compute (center, R, half_extents) for each link visual box.

Each box connects the joint-i origin to the joint-(i+1) origin. Box z-axis is aligned along the segment direction.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: List of (center, R, half_extents) tuples.

docs.title: Loading a preset
docs.text:
import forge3d.robot as f3r

arm = f3r.load("ur5", base_position=(0, 0, 0))

Available presets: "ur5" (UR5 6-DOF)

docs.text: Shape descriptors define the collision geometry of a body.

docs.text:
Collision / visual shape descriptor.

Create via factory methods::

Shape.box(size=(1, 1, 1))
Shape.sphere(radius=0.5)
Shape.capsule(radius=0.2, half_length=0.5)
Shape.convex_mesh(mesh_data)   # MeshData from load_obj()

docs.text:
box(size: Any = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0)) -> Shape

Box shape with half-extents derived from size.

docs.title: sphere staticmethod
docs.text:
sphere(radius: float = 0.5) -> Shape

Sphere shape.

docs.title: capsule staticmethod
docs.text:
capsule(
    radius: float = 0.2, half_length: float = 0.5
) -> Shape

Capsule shape (cylinder + hemispherical end-caps, axis = body +Z).

docs.title: convex_mesh staticmethod
docs.text:
convex_mesh(mesh_data: Any) -> Shape

Convex-hull collision shape from a MeshData object.

Load mesh data with::

from forge3d.io import load_obj
mesh = load_obj("model.obj")
shape = Shape.convex_mesh(mesh)

docs.title: Material
docs.text: Material describes the visual appearance (PBR).

docs.title: Material dataclass
docs.text:
Surface appearance for a rigid body.

Color can be a preset name (str) or an RGB tuple in [0, 1]. Preset names: 'default', 'red', 'blue', 'green', 'orange', 'ground', 'gold', 'white'. Use texture_path for an albedo image (PNG/JPEG, loaded at render time).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Material(color="red") Material(color=(0.9, 0.4, 0.1), roughness=0.3) Material(color="default", metallic=0.8, roughness=0.2) Material(texture_path="wall.png")

docs.title: Built-in colour presets
docs.text: Name RGB "default" (0.7, 0.7, 0.7) "red" (0.8, 0.2, 0.2) "blue" (0.2, 0.4, 0.8) "green" (0.2, 0.7, 0.2) "orange" (0.9, 0.5, 0.1) "gold" (1.0, 0.8, 0.0) "white" (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) "ground" (0.4, 0.35, 0.3)

docs.title: forge3d.World
docs.text: The central object of every simulation. It manages rigid bodies, advances physics, and produces SceneSnapshots for rendering.

docs.title: World
docs.text:
forge3d physics world — pygame-style public API.

Coordinate system: z-up, SI units. Start here::

world = forge3d.World()
world.add_ground()
box = world.add_box(size=(1, 1, 1), position=(0, 0, 5))
world.step()          # advances by default_dt=1/60 s
snap = world.snapshot()

The internal PhysicsWorld is accessible via world._physics for advanced use, but the public API never needs it.

docs.title: bodies property
docs.text:
bodies: list[Body]

All body handles currently in the world (order: insertion).

docs.title: time property
docs.text:
time: float

docs.title: step
docs.text:
step(dt: float | None = None) -> None

Advance simulation by dt seconds (default: 1/60 s).

  1. Flush per-body force/torque accumulators (apply_force / apply_torque).
  2. Apply per-body linear/angular damping.
  3. Physics step (gravity → contacts → impulses → position update).
  4. Apply weld constraints.
  5. Dispatch collision events (reuses cached contacts — no double detection).

docs.title: apply_impulse
docs.text:

Apply an instantaneous velocity impulse to body (Δv = impulse / mass).

Use this to apply per-frame forces from a game loop::

# Apply force F for time dt:
world.apply_impulse(ball, np.array([F_x, F_y, 0]) * dt)
world.step(dt)

weld(
    body: Body,
    anchor: Body,
    local_offset: Any = None,
    local_rotation: Any = None,
) -> None

Attach body kinematically to anchor (weld constraint).

After welding, body follows anchor rigidly each step(), preserving both relative position and relative orientation.

docs.title: Parameters

docs.title: add_joint
docs.text:
add_joint(
    joint_type: str,
    body_a: Body,
    body_b: Body | None = None,
    anchor_a: Any = (0.0, 0.0, 0.0),
    anchor_b: Any = (0.0, 0.0, 0.0),
    axis: Any = (0.0, 0.0, 1.0),
    limits: tuple[float, float] | None = None,
    motor_velocity: float | None = None,
    motor_max_torque: float = 10.0,
    stiffness: float = 100.0,
    damping: float = 5.0,
    rest_length: float = 1.0,
    target_distance: float = 1.0,
) -> Any

Add a joint constraint between two bodies.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default joint_type str

One of "fixed", "ball", "hinge", "prismatic", "distance", "spring".

required body_a Body

First body (required).

required body_b Body | None

Second body. If None, the joint anchors body_a to a world-fixed point (anchor_b in world frame).

None anchor_a Any

Attachment point in body_a local frame.

(0.0, 0.0, 0.0) anchor_b Any

Attachment point in body_b local frame (or world frame if body_b is None).

(0.0, 0.0, 0.0) axis Any

Hinge / slide axis in body_a local frame (used for "hinge" and "prismatic").

(0.0, 0.0, 1.0) limits tuple[float, float] | None

Angular limits (rad) for hinge or distance limits (m) for prismatic.

None motor_velocity float | None

Target velocity for hinge/prismatic motor.

None motor_max_torque float

Torque cap for hinge motor (N·m).

10.0 stiffness float

Spring constant k (N/m) for spring joint.

100.0 damping float

Damping coefficient c (N·s/m) for spring joint.

5.0 rest_length float

Natural spring length (m) for spring joint.

1.0 target_distance float

Target distance (m) for distance joint.

1.0

Returns:

Name Type Description A Any

class:forge3d.constraints.JointHandle (pass to

Any

meth:remove_joint to delete the joint).

Examples::

hinge = world.add_joint("hinge", door, frame,
                        anchor_a=(-0.5, 0, 0),
                        anchor_b=(0.5, 0, 0),
                        axis=(0, 0, 1))
spring = world.add_joint("spring", box, ceiling,
                         stiffness=200.0, damping=10.0,
                         rest_length=2.0)

docs.title: raycast
docs.text:
raycast(
    origin: Any, direction: Any, max_dist: float = 100.0
) -> Any | None

Cast a ray from origin along direction and return the first hit.

Tests the ray against all physics bodies (AABB then exact shape) and returns the closest intersection, or None if nothing is hit.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: origin : (3,) ray start in world frame (m). direction : (3,) ray direction — need not be normalised. max_dist : Maximum hit distance (m).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
A :class:RayHit namedtuple with fields (body, point, normal, distance) or None.

Example::

hit = world.raycast((0, 0, 5), (0, 0, -1), max_dist=10)
if hit:
    print(hit.body.name, hit.distance)

docs.title: Basic simulation loop
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))
ground = world.add_ground()
box = world.add_box(size=(1, 1, 1), position=(0, 0, 5), mass=1.0)

for _ in range(600):        # 10 seconds @ 60 Hz
    world.step(dt=1/60)

print(f"Final z: {box.position[2]:.3f} m")

docs.title: Static bodies (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
# All three create non-moving, collidable geometry — no mass needed
wall  = world.add_box(size=(10, 0.5, 3), position=(0, 5, 1.5), static=True)
cap   = world.add_capsule(radius=0.2, half_length=2, position=(0, 0, 3), static=True)
shelf = world.add_static_box(size=(4, 0.2, 0.1), position=(0, 0, 2))

docs.title: Managing bodies
docs.text:
world.remove(box)
world.clear(keep_statics=True)   # remove dynamics only
print(world.time)                # elapsed simulation time (s)

docs.title: Weld constraints with rotation (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
parent = world.add_box(size=(2, 2, 1), position=(0, 0, 2), mass=10)
child  = world.add_box(size=(0.5, 0.5, 0.5), position=(1, 0, 2), mass=0.5)

# Classic weld — child follows parent position and orientation
world.weld(child, parent)

# Weld with fixed relative rotation (e.g. angled windmill blade at 90°)
world.weld(blade, hub, local_rotation=[0.707, 0, 0, 0.707])  # [w, x, y, z]

    print(f"Contact point: {hit.point}")
    print(f"Surface normal: {hit.normal}")

docs.title: Tutorial 1 — Physics Simulation
docs.text: This tutorial walks through forge3d's physics features: gravity, rigid bodies, collision, and contact.

docs.title: Creating a world
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))  # z-up, SI units (m, kg, s)

The World manages all rigid bodies and advances the simulation. Coordinate system: z-up, right-hand. Units: metres, kilograms, seconds.

docs.title: Adding bodies
docs.text:
ground = world.add_ground()           # static infinite plane at z=0
box  = world.add_box(size=(1, 1, 1), position=(0, 0, 5), mass=1.0)
ball = world.add_sphere(radius=0.5,  position=(1, 0, 5), mass=0.5)
cap  = world.add_capsule(radius=0.2, half_length=0.5, position=(-1, 0, 5))

Every add_* call returns a Body handle you can query and modify later.

docs.title: Stepping the simulation
docs.text:
dt = 1.0 / 240          # 240 Hz recommended for contact
for _ in range(2400):   # 10 seconds
    world.step(dt=dt)

print(f"Box position: {box.position}")
print(f"Box velocity: {box.velocity}")

docs.title: Materials — friction & restitution
docs.text:
ice_box = world.add_box(
    size=(1, 1, 1),
    position=(0, 0, 3),
    mass=1.0,
    friction=0.05,          # very slippery
    restitution=0.0,        # no bounce
)

rubber_ball = world.add_sphere(
    radius=0.5,
    position=(0, 0, 5),
    mass=0.2,
    friction=0.8,
    restitution=0.9,        # very bouncy
)

docs.title: Applying forces and impulses
docs.text:
# Continuous force (applied every step)
world.step(dt=1/60)
box.apply_force((10, 0, 0))  # 10 N in +x — must be re-applied each step

# Instantaneous impulse (Δv = impulse / mass)
world.apply_impulse(ball, (0, 0, 5))  # kick ball up

# Teleport (instantly move)
world.teleport(box, position=(0, 0, 10))

docs.title: Querying body state
docs.text:
print(box.position)          # (3,) world-frame position
print(box.velocity)          # (3,) linear velocity m/s
print(box.orientation)       # (4,) quaternion [w, x, y, z]
print(box.angular_velocity)  # (3,) angular velocity rad/s
print(box.is_static)         # bool
print(box.mass)              # float, kg

docs.title: Removing bodies
docs.text:
world.remove(box)            # remove specific body
world.clear()                # remove all dynamic bodies
world.clear(keep_statics=False)  # remove everything

docs.title: Energy conservation check
docs.text:
import numpy as np

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))
ball = world.add_sphere(radius=0.5, position=(0, 0, 5), mass=1.0,
                         friction=0.0, restitution=1.0)

KE0 = 0.5 * ball.mass * np.dot(ball.velocity, ball.velocity)
PE0 = ball.mass * 9.81 * ball.position[2]
E0 = KE0 + PE0

for _ in range(600):
    world.step(dt=1/60)

KE = 0.5 * ball.mass * np.dot(ball.velocity, ball.velocity)
PE = ball.mass * 9.81 * ball.position[2]
E = KE + PE

print(f"Energy drift: {abs(E - E0) / E0 * 100:.2f}%")  # < 1%

docs.title: Static bodies (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
All body types now support static=True:

# Before v1.1.0 — required internal API
bid = world._physics.add_static_box(size=(10, 0.5, 3), ...)

# v1.1.0 — clean public API
wall  = world.add_box(size=(10, 0.5, 3), position=(0, 5, 1.5), static=True)
post  = world.add_capsule(radius=0.1, half_length=2.0, position=(3, 0, 2),
                          static=True)
shelf = world.add_static_box(size=(4, 0.2, 0.1), position=(0, 0, 2))

Static bodies have zero mass, are never moved by physics, and are automatically excluded from the outer collision loop — they don't slow down the simulation.

docs.title: Runtime physics properties (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
friction and restitution can now be changed after creation:

ice = world.add_box(size=(10, 10, 0.1), position=(0, 0, 0), static=True)
ice.friction = 0.02       # near-frictionless

rubber = world.add_sphere(radius=0.5, position=(0, 0, 3), mass=1)
rubber.restitution = 0.95  # very bouncy

docs.title: Per-body velocity damping (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
Instead of manually zeroing velocity each frame, use damping properties:

car = world.add_box(size=(2, 1, 0.5), position=(0, 0, 0.3), mass=20)

# Applied automatically every world.step()
car.linear_damping  = 1.0   # removes 63% of speed per second
car.angular_damping = 3.0   # removes 95% of spin per second

The formula is v_new = v * exp(-damping * dt), which is FPS-independent.

docs.title: Raycast (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
Find which body a ray hits:

# Cast downward from above to detect what's below
hit = world.raycast(origin=(0, 0, 10), direction=(0, 0, -1), max_dist=20)
if hit:
    print(f"Hit: {hit.body.name}")
    print(f"Point: {hit.point}")
    print(f"Normal: {hit.normal}")
    print(f"Distance: {hit.distance:.2f} m")

Supported shapes: sphere, box (OBB), capsule (approximate).

docs.title: Tutorial 2 — Realtime & HQ Rendering
docs.text: forge3d has two renderers sharing the same API: a realtime OpenGL rasteriser and a high-quality software ray-tracer. Both consume a SceneSnapshot — the physics code never changes.

docs.title: Realtime viewer
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World()
world.add_ground()
world.add_box(size=(1, 1, 1), position=(0, 0, 3), mass=1.0,
              material=f3d.Material(color="red"))

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world, width=1280, height=720)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step(dt=1/60)
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: Viewer controls (default)
docs.text: Action Control Orbit Left-drag Pan Middle-drag Zoom Scroll Close Esc or window X

docs.title: Camera
docs.text:
cam = f3d.OrbitCamera(
    target=(0, 0, 1),     # look-at point
    distance=10,           # metres from target
    azimuth=45,            # degrees
    elevation=30,          # degrees above horizontal
    fov_deg=60,
)

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world)
while viewer.is_open:
    inp = viewer.input
    # Right-drag to orbit
    if inp.mouse_button(1):
        dx, dy = inp.mouse_delta()
        cam.rotate(d_azimuth=dx * 0.5, d_elevation=-dy * 0.5)
    cam.zoom(inp.scroll_delta() * 0.1)
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot())
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()

docs.title: Materials
docs.text:
# Built-in colour presets
f3d.Material(color="red")
f3d.Material(color="blue")
f3d.Material(color="gold")
f3d.Material(color="ground")

# RGB tuple [0, 1]
f3d.Material(color=(0.9, 0.4, 0.1))

# PBR parameters
f3d.Material(color="white", roughness=0.1, metallic=0.9)  # mirror-like

# Texture
f3d.Material(texture_path="assets/textures/brick.png")

docs.title: High-quality offline recording
docs.text:
import forge3d as f3d

world = f3d.World(gravity=(0, 0, -9.81))
world.add_ground(material=f3d.Material(color="ground", roughness=0.8))
ball = world.add_sphere(
    radius=0.4, position=(0, 0, 4.4), mass=1.0, restitution=0.8,
    material=f3d.Material(color="orange"))
world.set_camera(position=(4, -7, 3), target=(0, 0, 1))

rec = f3d.Recorder(
    world,
    mode="hq",
    resolution=(1920, 1080),
    samples=64,           # rays per pixel — higher = better quality, slower
    output="bounce.mp4",
)
rec.run(duration=3.0, dt=1/240, fps=60)

samples=64 gives cinema-quality anti-aliasing; samples=4 is fast preview.

docs.title: Recording a policy rollout
docs.text:
from apps.robot_rl.envs.reach_env import ReachEnv
from stable_baselines3 import PPO

env   = ReachEnv(render_mode=None)
model = PPO.load("reach_policy")

rec = f3d.Recorder(world, mode="hq", output="rollout.mp4")
rec.run_policy(model, env, duration=5.0)

docs.title: Heightfield terrain rendering (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
Before v1.1.0, world.add_terrain() was collision-only — the terrain was invisible. In v1.1.0, terrain automatically appears as a shaded triangle mesh with shadow casting:

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
heights = (
    3.0 * np.sin(np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 48))[:, None] *
          np.cos(np.linspace(0, 3*np.pi, 48))[None, :] +
    rng.uniform(-0.3, 0.3, (48, 48))
).astype(np.float32)
heights = np.clip(heights - heights.min(), 0, 8)

world.add_terrain(
    heights=heights,
    cell_size=2.5,
    origin=(-60, -60, 0),
    material=f3d.Material(color=(0.28, 0.42, 0.16), roughness=0.95),
)

viewer = f3d.Viewer(world)
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()    # terrain rendered as smooth mesh with normals and shadows

The terrain mesh is generated lazily on the first render call, cached, and reused every frame.

docs.title: HUD text overlay (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
Viewer.draw_text() renders a text overlay after the 3D scene:

score = 0
while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.draw()                                 # 3D scene

    viewer.draw_text(f"Score: {score}", x=10, y=10, size=24)
    viewer.draw_text("PAUSE", x=640, y=360,
                     size=48, color=(1, 0.8, 0), anchor="center")
    viewer.draw_text("ESC to quit", x=1270, y=10,
                     size=18, anchor="topright")
Parameter Default Description x, y 10, 10 Pixel position size 20 Font size (px) color (1,1,1) RGB in [0,1] bg_alpha 0.6 Background box opacity anchor "topleft" "topleft" / "center" / "topright"

docs.title: FollowCamera — local frame (v1.1.0)
docs.text:
For vehicle games, use frame="local" so the camera always stays behind the body:

car = world.add_box(size=(2, 1, 0.5), position=(0, 0, 0.3), mass=20)

cam = f3d.FollowCamera(
    car,
    offset=(-8, 0, 3),      # 8 m behind, 3 m up (car's local frame)
    frame="local",
    smoothing_hz=8,          # snappy tracking
    fov_deg=60,
)

while viewer.is_open:
    world.step()
    viewer.set_camera(cam.to_snapshot(dt=viewer.dt))
    viewer.draw()

With frame="world" (default), the camera offset is always world-aligned regardless of where the car points. With frame="local", the camera rotates with the car.

docs.title: Tutorial 3 — Robot Arm (FK / IK)
docs.text: forge3d includes a UR5 6-DOF robot arm preset with forward kinematics, inverse kinematics, and Jacobian computation.

docs.title: Loading a robot
docs.text:
import numpy as np
import forge3d as f3d
import forge3d.robot as f3r

world = f3d.World()
world.add_ground()

arm = f3r.load("ur5", base_position=(0, 0, 0))
world.add(arm)

f3r.load("ur5") creates a UR5 with DH parameters matching the real robot.

docs.title: Setting joint angles
docs.text:
# Home position
arm.set_joints([0.0, -np.pi/2, np.pi/2, -np.pi/2, -np.pi/2, 0.0])
world.step()

# Read end-effector pose
ee_pos, ee_rot = arm.ee_pose()
print(f"End-effector position: {ee_pos.round(4)}")
print(f"End-effector orientation (quat): {ee_rot.round(4)}")

docs.title: Forward kinematics
docs.text:
q = np.array([0.5, -1.0, 1.5, -0.5, -0.5, 0.0])
arm.set_joints(q)
world.step()

pos, rot = arm.ee_pose()

The FK uses the Denavit-Hartenberg convention with all 6 joints.

docs.title: Jacobian
docs.text:
q = arm.joint_angles()
J = arm.jacobian(q)         # (6, 6) geometric Jacobian
J_pos = J[:3, :]            # (3, 6) position Jacobian
J_rot = J[3:, :]            # (3, 6) rotation Jacobian

docs.title: Velocity control
docs.text:
# Move end-effector at 0.1 m/s in +z
target_ee_vel = np.array([0, 0, 0.1, 0, 0, 0])  # [vx, vy, vz, wx, wy, wz]
q = arm.joint_angles()
J = arm.jacobian(q)

# Pseudoinverse joint velocity
J_pinv = np.linalg.pinv(J)
dq = J_pinv @ target_ee_vel
arm.set_joints(q + dq * 0.01)   # dt = 0.01 s

docs.title: Sweeping joints (video)
docs.text:
rec = f3d.Recorder(world, mode="hq", output="sweep.mp4")

def sweep_policy(obs):
    t = world.time
    q = np.zeros(6)
    q[0] = np.sin(t)      # shoulder pan
    q[1] = -np.pi/2 + 0.5 * np.sin(2 * t)
    return q

rec.run_policy(sweep_policy, env=None, duration=5.0)

docs.title: Tutorial 4 — Reinforcement Learning
docs.text: forge3d provides Gymnasium-compatible environments for robot learning. Training runs headless (no renderer) and is fast enough for CPU-only machines with JAX JIT+vmap.

docs.title: ReachEnv — robot reaching
docs.text:
from apps.robot_rl.envs.reach_env import ReachEnv
import gymnasium as gym

env = ReachEnv(render_mode=None)   # headless (fast)
obs, info = env.reset()
print(f"Obs shape: {obs.shape}")   # (12,)
print(f"Action space: {env.action_space}")  # Box(-1, 1, (6,))

docs.title: Observation (12 dims)
docs.text: Index Description 0–5 Joint angles q₀–q₅ (rad) 6–8 End-effector position (m) 9–11 Target position (m)

docs.title: Action (6 dims)
docs.text: Delta joint angles Δq ∈ [−1, 1] rad/step.

docs.title: Training with SB3
docs.text:
from stable_baselines3 import PPO
from apps.robot_rl.envs.reach_env import ReachEnv

env   = ReachEnv(render_mode=None)
model = PPO("MlpPolicy", env, verbose=1, n_steps=2048, batch_size=64)
model.learn(total_timesteps=200_000)
model.save("reach_policy")

docs.title: Render modes
docs.text:
# Headless (training)
env = ReachEnv(render_mode=None)

# RGB array (video capture)
env = ReachEnv(render_mode="rgb_array")
frame = env.render()      # (H, W, 3) uint8

# Human (interactive window)
env = ReachEnv(render_mode="human")

docs.title: PickPlaceEnv — pick and place
docs.text:
from apps.robot_rl.envs.pick_place_env import PickPlaceEnv

env = PickPlaceEnv(render_mode=None)
obs, info = env.reset()
print(f"Obs shape: {obs.shape}")   # (18,)

The pick-and-place environment uses the weld kinematic constraint to simulate grasping.

docs.title: JAX batch stepping — 2,000× speedup
docs.text:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from forge3d.sim.jax_batch import batch_reach_reset, batch_reach_step

key = jax.random.PRNGKey(42)
q, tgt, obs = batch_reach_reset(key, n_envs=256)

# One batched step across 256 environments
q, obs, rew, done = batch_reach_step(q, tgt, jnp.zeros((256, 6)))

JAX JIT + vmap runs 256 environments in a single kernel call.

docs.title: Learning dashboard
docs.text:
python apps/robot_rl/dashboard.py --log-dir logs/reach/

Displays a matplotlib dashboard with reward curve, success rate, and episode length.
